content.full_text:
April 20, 1978


Mr. Peter Hanauer, Treasurer
California Group Against Smoking Pollution
P.O. Box 1061
Berkeley, California 94701

Dear Mr. Hanauer:

This letter is in response to our telephone conversation on
April 12, 1978 regarding the status for filing campaign statements
of the California Group Against Smoking Pollution (GASP). The
specific issue presented is whether GASP is a committee that receives contributions as defined in Government Code Section 82013(a).
GASP has been filing as a committee as defined in Government Code
Section 82013(c) because it has made expenditures in excess of
$5,000 in support of the Clean Indoor Air Act. The resolution of
this issue will determine which campaign statement forms GASP
must file and what information must be disclosed.

My understanding of the facts regarding the operation of GASP
is as follows:

- GASP is an ongoing membership organization created approximately two years ago to protect the rights of non-smokers.

- A great majority of GASP's members pay dues of $10 - $15
per year.

- GASP helped establish the Clean Indoor Air Committee the
primary committee supporting the Clean Indoor Air Act, an
initiative measure that will appear on the November 1978
ballot.

- Except for contributions to and expenditures at the behest
of the Clean Indoor Air Committee, GASP has no prior
history of making campaign contributions.

- GASP has not mentioned the Clean Indoor Air Act on its
membership forms or on literature sent to prospective
members.

"Mr. Peter Hanauer                                    -2-                          April 20, 1978

"Contribution" is defined in Government Code Section 82015 as
"... a payment ... except to the extent full and adequate consider-
ation is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circum-
stances that it is not made for political purposes..."

The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted an adminis-
trative regulation, 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18215, to clarify the
definition of contribution. Subsection (d) of this regulation states
in part:

    "... "contribution" includes any monetary or non-
    monetary payment ... that is received by any
    person or organization other than a candidate,
    controlled committee, official committee of a
    political party, or organization formed or exis-
    ting primarily for political purposes if the
    payment is "earmarked" for the making of contri-
    butions or expenditures. A payment is "earmarked"
    when, at the time of making the payment, the donor
    knows or has reason to know that the payment or
    the funds with which the funds will be commingled
    will be used to make contributions ... factors
    relevant to a determination that the donor has
    reason to know ... include but are not limited
    to the established practice of the person or
    organization with respect to expenditures and
    contributions and any representations made when
    the payment is solicited.

In accordance with this regulation, the staff has determined,
based on our understanding of the facts as outlined above, that
GASP is not receiving "contributions" as defined in Government Code
Section 82015, from its members at the present time. This deter-
mination is based on the fact that GASP has no established practice
with respect to expenditures and contributions and no representa-
tions have been made by GASP that a portion of the dues will be
used to make contributions or expenditures. Therefore, if GASP
does not change its manner in which it solicits members, a portion
of each member's dues will not represent contributions at any time
prior to the November 1978 election. Accordingly, GASP is consid-
ered a "major donor" committee, as defined in Government Code
Section 82013(c), and must continue to file Form 461 on the dates
required and disclose all expenditures and contributions made for
political purposes.

If, however, GASP does change its method of membership solici-
tation so that new members are informed that a portion of their
dues will be used to make contributions or expenditures (for the
initiative or other political purposes), then a portion of each
member's dues will represent reportable contributions for a political
purpose to GASP. In addition, if GASP continues to make contri-
tions or expenditures after the November 1978 election then GASP

Mr. Peter Hanauer                                                    -3-                                    April 20, 1978

will have an established practice with respect to making contributions and expenditures and, again, a portion of each member's dues to GASP will represent reportable contributions solicited for a political purpose.

In the event that GASP does start receiving contributions for political purposes from its members, as described above, then GASP must file a Statement of Organization (Form 410) with the Secretary of State within ten days of its receipt of $500 in reportable contributions. GASP would then be considered a "recipient committee", as defined in Government Code Section 82013(a), and must file either Form 420 or 450, as appropriate, and disclose a portion of each member's dues as contributions received for a political purpose and disclose all expenditures and contributions made.

If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,


Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative

AH:bw

cc: Dick Bryant
    L.A. County Registrar-Recorder's Office
    
    Secretary of State's Office

For use by: (1) persons who have made expenditures or contributions totaling
$5,000 or more in a calendar year directly or on behalf of any candidate,
officeholder or committee; or, (2) persons who have made independent expendit-
ures on behalf of a candidate, officeholder or ballot measure in the amount of
$500 or more.

2                          CALIFORNIA GROUP AGAINST SMOKING POLLUTION         MARCH/APRIL 1978


DEPT. OF DEFENSE

The Department of Defense has come out
with a new policy on smoking that is as good
as or better than most civilian companies.

The policy was adopted November 15, 1977
by Deputy Secretary of Defense Charles W.
Duncan. The policy affects all DOD occupied buildings and facilities.

The following are the provisions in brochure
form by the GASP Legal Fund and will be of
great use to those working in the Armed
Forces or their families who wish to use it
to restrain their right to breathe clean
air.

"No Smoking Policy - November 15, 1977

In an environment reasonably free of contamination, and recognizes the right to
smoke. "Provided such action does not endanger the health or property, cause discomfort
to others, or interfere with the rights
of others." All military personnel are reminded that these rights are cited.

The policy includes:

1) Audit before-1025 Nonsmokes, post signs,
   remove ashtrays

2) Eating facilities - 1 non-smoking area
   based on 1 non-smoking customers served.

3) Elevators - 100% No Smoking

4) Shuttle Vehicles - 100% no smoking

5) Medical Care facilities - smoking in
   staff lounges, private offices and
   patient waiting areas. Visitors must
   be confined in visitor waiting area only
   where there is no established prohibition.

6) Conference & classrooms - 100% no smoking

7) Work areas - smoking only if official
   duties require such areas (if considered permitted offices of COMSEC
   will and provide an environ-
   mental arrangement.

8) Corridors, lounges and restrooms -
   except in designated areas facilities
   from smoking allowed.

This policy has NOT been enforced by GAF
Legal Fund yet but it is in the process.
Those who find non-compliance in military
facilities should file formal complaints
sent to their superior when they are try-
ing to enforce their right to breathe clean
air. If the Department of Defense accom-
plishes its non-enforcement of 100% of this
policy should be available at your location.


     The Respirator is published by

     Pollution, P. O. Box 1061,
     Berkeley, CA 94701-1794.

HARASSMENT
ON THE JOB

I work in the Office of the Staff Nurse
(University, President of San Francisco, California). Over a year ago I had a "frank discussion" with one of my co-supervisors re our exposure to cigarette smoke. In response, the immediate supervisor (CJ Lowell (510)593-9632) do something, not even bothering to answer my last letter.

After obtaining the name of regulation let "this year will cease in effect. In comparison
with those of other federal and state offices, it is certainly not strict, yet it will suffice for me to remove smoke from a work area under an environment that is healthful".

The procedure I followed (consulting with the Staff Nurse & my union reps) led to a union meeting with supervisor to try to establish and implement a harassment and anti-retaliation clause. In this working area he even told me he read letters I get. Through a mailed several lines, he called me into his office and said what I needed. I told him if he felt I was wasting company time through union business, we could on feminist "stuff" and we letter allegations. I emphasized that I was working toward a healthful environment and he mentioned something about "insubordination" as a working area.

He realized that another individual was also working in same "silver and the same" complaints. I realized that since I did not name that person, I suspected harassment for a work area and that the other individual apparently did not have an allergies. CJ Lowell then said that the problem should be written in his maid as ask outside organizations to get involved, particularly City Hall. "I don't know what their motives is can't but we're care less about another 'frustration'."

An outcome of the "discussion" resulted in no work since being cleared from the half-inch to smoke... This "cleared" was in my workplace, a windowless office, and also at my desk. Needless to say I won't sit much air either.

As further harassment, CJ Lowell has directed my mail deadlines on all my work, not telling me the right way to go some of the work at [illegible] and [illegible] later. This could just as well be used as collecting the mail and returning it early before I can read it. My family has not been able to communicate with me at work since calls are now routed not through to me either with my immediate boss.

Her room: "former aide to ARTY secure 'im."

FOR YOUR
INFORMATION

Many of you have been misundirected re "clean labor" initiative. referring to the Clean Indoor Air Initiative" or the "CCAI petition."

Although Californians for Clean Indoor Air are supporters of the clean indoor air initiative, we are providing most of the muscle and support for passage this year.

Please study the following abbreviations and make note you use the proper one when referring to the various initiatives:

CCIA - Californians for Clean Indoor Air - sponsored with C.I.A.I.

CIAG - Clean Indoor Air Committee- This official sponsoring org and representing the initiative

CIAA - Clean Indoor Air initiative (official name of the initiative measure we are trying to get voted into law).

CIAI - Clean Indoor Air Initiative- Informal name for the initiative or effort.

It is probably best never to use the phrase "CIA initiative" as we have nothing to do with the Central Intelligence Agency.

Remember, the CIAG is the steering committee for the CIAI and is actively championing the CIAA.

(Same of San Francisco) Michael Vong, earlier this week with David Carmichael (board member) called to alert us that some anti-labor forces groups plan a direct mail campaign opposing our pro-labor and health lobbying. In addition the media called Lowell, but on the line was Williams, the original [illegible].

Victoria Deloin

CALIFORNIA GROUP AGAINST SWCING POLLUTION          RESUSCITATOR          MARCH-APRIL 1978

Chapter News

EAST BAY                                                                                    Diablo
                                                                                            Valley
CALENDAR OF EVENTS

April 1 - Berkeley Hills
April 8 - Centennial Picnic
April 11 - Dr. Joel Fort at East Bay meeting
April 12 - Berkeley City meeting

East Bay GASP is now directing its energy toward yet another battle in the seemingly endless war against pollution. Berkeley City Council amended the ordinance on enforcement of

"Section 1.07  INFRACTIONS AND PENALTIES

(a) When a person is arrested for violating declared to be an infraction,
the person shall not be held or
be released by citation in accordance
with procedures set forth in section
853.8(a) of the Penal Code."

This language came from GASP. On Saturday, April 1, we are going to visit some businesses in Berkeley to help them understand what the ordinance says and whether they have signs posted, and if not, what they should do. A detailed report will be sent to the GASP office at 1922 McGee at the following Saturday, April 8.

Anita Simons, our Diablo coordinator, is directing the survey of 1932 McGee around 9:30 p.m.
We have to obey the regulations that each law is enforceable and derived by a majority of the people and is encouraged. We strongly encourage all of you to get involved. The ordinance comes up for review which will probably means it will be reviewed by the City Council sometime before July 1 of this year. The San Francisco Foundation has granted the City $35,000 to fund a Smoke Abatement Program which will be spent on his Precaution Students at the Wright Institute. The study, which will include air quality, noise and housing around 30 Berkeley businesses, will be used by the City Council when it considers ordinance review. East Bay G.A.S.P. will also be there to impart information and data collected.

On Saturday, April 8, the city of Berkeley will be celebrating with a parade starting at Shattuck and Parker. We will all be a part of "March of Importance". East Bay GASP will be marching in the parade, which is enclosed Public places in Berkeley. If anyone has any interest in getting involved in the float contact your GASP office (548-1748) for further details.

Joel Fort, M.D. will speak on "Smoking and the Lung" at a special meeting which will be held in the hospitality Room of San Francisco's best at the University of California at Berkeley.

At our March meeting, members have a representative from the Berkeley Lung Association English Dept. present a ordinance enforcing any violation. The ordinance passed first was set up on the mail. In fact, we are now working at other than what we get. We would like to offer Last Bay GASP. There has not been complete enforcement to force businesses to enforce. We strongly urge members of GASP to keep an eye out for violations of what they see and should do when they see a violation. Here's a chance to find out how to do it! Please attend.

San Diego

The anniversary and victory celebration meeting on March 4 was a huge success. Even with the rain and the hard-to-find community house, we had a very good turnout to celebrate our victory. Our aircraft thanks go to Bill Gordon for enlightening and entertaining us on such a momentous occasion. As Bill said, "Let's do it again." We're really proud and looking forward to victory in November."

One more piece of good news - we were able to attend to pick up his award, his office did send a telegram.

Other exciting awards for their outstanding leadership in the quest against air pollution were Chuck Center, Henry's Restaurant, Del Hood Construction, Dave Koch - Alum Rock (Capt. and Ed Simmons - both in the initial association), Dr. Olin White, Channel 39, San Diego - both unable to attend), and Supervisor Jim Bates.

The winners of the local initiative contest sponsored by Susan and Bill Ballard were Kathy Burke won $50 which she said she was donating to GASP (thank you Kathy! You were a hard act to follow!), Chris Westlake won $35 which she donated to the Sierra Club, and Mary Bricker won $15. Thank to the winners and the Ballards.

Anita Simons, County Coordinator, thanked all those who had done so much during the initiative campaign, in particular the officers who never faltered and many local supporters and friends of them. Let Coskunik, Betty Heitman and Heather Knudston. Special tribute was paid to Max Treves who was presented with an award of merit and also a special certificate and a mini-crown for the "King of Smoke Gathering" who collected over 21,000 of his own.

New officers - Secretary, Carol Westerberg and Treasurer, Dick Cooper were introduced. A good time was had by all and the refreshments were great and plentiful.

The next monthly meeting will be Thursday, April 13, 7:30 p.m. at the Lions Association. There will be elections for the P.O. Association - probably something humorous on the smoking ordinance. Plans for legislative campaign strategy and fund raising will be discussed. We are also in need of a new historian to keep the GASP scrapbook, so an interested volunteer is indeed to continue with the job.

See you all on the 13th.

Anita Simons

Diablo
Valley

CHICKEN GROW
SUNDAY, APRIL 16

The success of our last two pot-luck dinners has and we'll serve home-cooked chicken in an informal setting. It's amazing how many new members and creative ideas can be produced by a fried chicken!

All members of the family will be welcome to our 6th Annual Sunday Chicken Dinner courtesy of Kentucky Fried Chicken.

Others are invited in one of our members' home for good conversation, good food and strategy for the future of Diablo Valley GASP or just meet new/old friends, then come to:

55 Stanton Ave
Orinda, CA

RSVP
276-1681

Carol Olswart
and
Al Dalton

SUNDAY, 4/16
6:00 p.m.

SAN JOAQUIN COUNTY

Dr. Jay Smith, Northern California Coordinator, was the main speaker at our March 26 meeting. Two dozen members and friends - we had a great speech, and we all left wishing to capture 300,000 tons of air in the Clean Air Initiative.

The Board discussed helping to recruit a speaker for our March 24 meeting. Please note the change of our regular meeting date! The 4th Tuesday of each month but starting in April the 2nd Tuesday of Hammer Lane and Pacific Avenue.

Plans for our April meeting are incomplete. We meet at this writing on Santa's even held our March morning seeing we expect to be providing information about the CTIA proposition in November.

Many of our members and students are already helping us. We are also on our Civil Registry Team that experienced in a political campaign and one is even helping us with GASP for their projects.

Wish you get a good idea for the campaign, telephone me at 478-1301.

Ken Sleighley

CALIFORNIA GROUP AGAINST SMOKING POLLUTION                NEWSLETTER                MARCH-APRIL 1978

Clean Indoor Air

[Left column contains a list of names - transcription of names:]
[Multiple names listed vertically - partially legible]

ATTENTION!
The main office is embarking upon a new questionnaire to each chapter requesting an update on chapter officers for our mailing lists. If you haven't received a questionnaire and it turned out that we did not have your address, please contact us. All chapters should return the completed forms to our office as soon as possible.

LETTERS NEEDED

Brian Witkin of WOODBRIDGE in Philadelphia, New Jersey, informs us that:

"Joseph Califano has proposed a comprehensive smoking control program for the Department of Health. It is the largest single action the government has taken to reverse the smoking trend in this country.

"A Congressional subcommittee is debating the funding of the program at the present time. However, the Tobacco Institute has mounted an impressive lobbying campaign to argue against such funding. Delaying members of Congress need support from constituents with letters on this vital and necessary government program.

WRITING IT: Every day thousands of young people still start smoking. Their support for maintaining this federal program is crucial, and it will require continued grassroots action on smoking overall.

"The members of the sub-committee are:
Henry A. Waxman - California
William Lehman - Kentucky
Neal Smith - Iowa
James J. Florio - New Jersey
David Obey - Wisconsin
Edward J. Markey - Massachusetts
Louis Stokes - Ohio
Joseph Early - Massachusetts
Albert Gore, Jr. - Tennessee
Robert N. Giaimo - Illinois
George E. Danielson - Illinois

WRITE TO THEM AT: House Office Building
Washington, D.C. 20515"

Some of these people to support Mondale's "Rights" so get your letters off to all of them considering if possible.

Who brought in the early

signatures.

Thank you for all
your assistance
that precious time,
when were raised out,
tested out,
and spread.
The debt can never be paid,
But it can be acknowledged.

Lile Aragones and Elaine Hollingsworth
at work in Los Angeles: Century City.
(PHOTO BY ELAINE GALAR)

JERRY NEEDS PEN PALS

We need 20 get Governor Brown to support the Clean Indoor Air Bill in the State Legislature. You can write to him, urging his support. Letters work! So won't you support Jerry? It's his campaign that has us. Send a letter flood his office with letters, telegrams, and phone calls.

Governor Jerry Brown
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

[Signature]

PLEAS PAY UP!

We still Thank you for support of California GASP. During the signet gathering campaign, we came out so far we can't even pay our bills paid. Some of our members have not paid their membership dues GASP GETS TO THAT CALIFORNIA GASP. CHECK IS BLANK. SEND IN YOUR DUES!

SAN FERNANDO VALLEY

San Fernando Valley will hold its next air quality action meeting at 7:30 P.M. Tuesday, April 11, at San Fernando Valley State College, 18111 Nordhoff St., Northridge, California. (North of San Fernando Valley Blvd., Sherman Oaks. Call 213-983-3396 for more information.

GEF AND GLF

The Educational Foundation and GASP Legal Fund are flourishing this spring. Both are at work promoting informational materials to reveal the damaging effects of tobacco smoke on nonsmokers. GASP continues to develop new educational materials. GLF is currently working on a case dealing with smoking in the car. Costs of smokingessences [illegible] we're encountering on the foundation are tax deductible, and their expanding work load requires more money to keep them moving.

The Foundation is also planning to prepare an educational file on nonsmokers' rights. Two volunteer lawyers have offered to take on nonsmokers' right cases when both organizations [illegible] fund need more based money simply to get proposals written in draft form. Right grants from other foundations at this time. Some of these tasks will take thousands of dollars but their educational value will be immeasurable.

A tax-deductible donation to the GASP Educational Foundation and/or the GASP Legal Fund at P. O. Box 1061, Berkeley, CA 94701.

and Community

Under EPA's other policy, this could be a non-smokier and be acceptable.
I was at last smoking!

SMOKE SIGNALS - JANUARY 1978

The True Cost of Smoking
Compiled by GASP

This year begins another chapter in the long history of cigarette smoking's toll on Americans and the nation. And a historical smoking trend look at the past year's events shows clearly that the cost of smoking is greater than anybody can calculate while its supporters ignore the evidence.

Exactly 30 billion packs of cigarettes are sold in the U.S. annually, which means we're spending more for this single habit than for education or health care for most of our population. Even more than $4 million under the six of every four years on smoking-related disease.

These statistics on smoking and health, prepared from recent medical studies, present a bleak picture. Of all preventable deaths in the U.S., smoking accounts for some 14 percent. In a landmark decision, Congress admitted that since 1964, a package warning on cigarette labels would be needed.

Now, a government report of last month shows that between the ages of 25 and 65, smokers die at almost twice the rate of non-smokers. One study states that, between ages 45 and 65, smoking men were 6 times more likely to be hospitalized than non-smokers. And smokers were absent from work an average of 11 more days per year.

While "freedom of choice" advocates argue that smokers have the right to smoke, the cost of smoking to the non-smoker has been well documented. One study disclosed that nonsmokers living with smokers had three times higher rates of lung cancer than those living with non-smokers. And children in homes where parents smoked showed significantly more respiratory illness than children from non-smoking homes.

It's just a matter of time when the economic and health costs of smoking will force the government to take action, much as it did with other public health hazards.

PRINTER NEEDED

Many thanks to Rodney Frenchland for the time he has devoted to our print shop. He has put in endless hours and simply cannot continue. We desperately need someone who loves this work and is willing to it. Is there anyone who lives relatively close to Berkeley who has printing experience or who would like to learn to run our print press? Will you give us a day or more per week? Is there anyone interested, seriously, in learning how to run a printing press? If interested, call Zoe Vincker at [illegible].

Growing Clan of Smokers Who Quit

By Paul Taylor

People mentioning the fact that one in five American smokers quit smoking is bad news. The numbers are encouraging.

Statistics released at a hearing concerning state health policy this week revealed that 44 million Americans have quit smoking. The smoking ban in the tobaccos habit. They were nudged or more than just habit by either dramatic health warnings issued by the American Health Foundation, Evora Kempe and Shoya Higaki.

"We realize a smoker's eye view on why people might want to quit," said Paul Taylor, a San Francisco tobacco advertising consultant. "The reasons given are personal economic reasons, health reasons, and safety to others."

Although no one is certain what is changing these smoking habits of the non-smoking campaign a little too strong, they point out the ads for tobacco are in striking contradiction to the ads for non-smoking anti-smoking and other health concerns.

However, ASH Director Banzhaf says this is only a beginning. He still predicts that unless the people back anti-smoking and non-smokers throughout the country, smoking will continue to be a major public health problem.

"Until antismoking sentiment is strong enough to withstand these pressures from the tobacco forces, there won't be much change," Banzhaf says.

[Image of person offering cigarette to another person]

"Cigarettes advertise: that's what advertising does. Until we ban advertising, the smoking problem will continue."

SAN FRANCISCO SUNDAY CHRONICLE & EXAMINER
1/15/78

embedding.qa_text: April 20, 1978 Mr. Peter Hanauer, Treasurer California Group Against Smoking Pollution P.O. Box 1061 Berkeley, California 94701 Dear Mr. Hanauer: This letter is in response to our telephone conversation on April 12, 1978 regarding the status for filing campaign statements of the California Group Against Smoking Pollution (GASP). The specific issue presented is whether GASP is a committee that receives contributions as defined in Government Code Section 82013(a). GASP has been filing as a committee as defined in Government Code Section 82013(c) because it has made expenditures in excess of $5,000 in support of the Clean Indoor Air Act. The resolution of this issue will determine which campaign statement forms GASP must file and what information must be disclosed. My understanding of the facts regarding the operation of GASP is as follows: - GASP is an ongoing membership organization created approximately two years ago to protect the rights of non-smokers. - A great majority of GASP's members pay dues of $10 - $15 per year. - GASP helped establish the Clean Indoor Air Committee the primary committee supporting the Clean Indoor Air Act, an initiative measure that will appear on the November 1978 ballot. - Except for contributions to and expenditures at the behest of the Clean Indoor Air Committee, GASP has no prior history of making campaign contributions. - GASP has not mentioned the Clean Indoor Air Act on its membership forms or on literature sent to prospective members. "Contribution" is defined in Government Code Section 82015 as "...a payment ... except to the extent full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes..." The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted an administrative regulation, 2 Cal Adm. Code Section 18215, to clarify the definition of contribution. Subsection (d) of this regulation states in part: ... "contribution" includes any monetary or non-monetary payment ... that is received by any person or organization other than a candidate, controlled committee, official committee of a political party, or organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes if the payment is "earmarked" for the making of contributions or expenditures. A payment is "earmarked" when, at the time of making the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or the funds with which the funds will be commingled will be used to make contributions ... factors relevant to a determination that the donor has reason to know ... include but are not limited to the established practice of the person or organization with respect to expenditures and contributions and any representations made when the payment is solicited. In accordance with this regulation, the staff has determined, based on our understanding of the facts as outlined above, that GASP is not receiving "contributions" as defined in Government Code Section 82015, from its members at the present time. This determination is based on the fact that GASP has no established practice with respect to expenditures and contributions and
